title: 8  Blackboard Ultra: Deploying Quarto Content to Your Course Shell
section: 8.5 Course Structure Template: The Full-Year Model
text:
8.5 Course Structure Template: The Full-Year Model
Now that you understand the setup process, let’s detail the specific structure you’ve developed for your courses. This structure balances content organization with manageable maintenance and clear student navigation.

8.5.1 Overview of Your Structure
Your model organizes each module around a core concept or unit, with distinct folders for content delivery, learning materials, and assignments:






Screenshot needed: Blackboard Ultra course content panel showing the full module list — all modules collapsed, so the reader can see the top-level naming convention (“Module 1 — Topic”, “Module 2 — Topic”, etc.).
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-module-list-overview.png



Module [Number] - [Topic Name]/
├── Wrap-Up Content (overview/summary file)
├── Highlight Page (special HTML content)
├── Module [X] Lecture 1/
│   ├── Lecture Notes
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Tutorial
├── Module [X] Lecture 2/
│   ├── Lecture Notes
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Tutorial
└── Deliverables/
    ├── Assignment
    ├── Lab 1
    ├── Lab 2
    ├── Participation 1
    ├── Participation 2
    └── Project Step [N]


8.5.2 Understanding Each Component

8.5.2.1 Wrap-Up Content
Purpose: Provides a module overview, learning objectives, and synthesis of concepts.
Content Examples: - Summary of key concepts - Module learning outcomes - Prerequisites - Recommended reading order - Time estimates for completion
In Quarto: Create this as a dedicated .qmd file that renders as your module introduction. For example:
# Module 3: Advanced Data Visualization

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this module, you will:
- Understand principles of effective data visualization
- Create interactive visualizations with D3.js
- Apply color theory and accessibility standards

## Module Overview

This module explores how to transform raw data into compelling visual narratives...
In Blackboard: Link this as the first item in each module. Students see it immediately upon entering, setting context and expectations.






Screenshot needed: A single module expanded in Blackboard Ultra, with the Wrap-Up content item highlighted at the top — shows the “first item” position and the document icon.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-module-expanded-wrapup.png





8.5.2.2 Highlight Page
Purpose: A special HTML page highlighting a particular concept, real-world application, or student spotlight.
Examples: - “Industry Spotlight: How Netflix Uses Data Science” - “Case Study: Successful Project Implementation” - “Student Spotlight: Award-Winning Project” - “Emerging Trends: AI in Healthcare”
Creation: This can be a more visually-rich Quarto file, incorporating: - Images and diagrams - Embedded videos - Interactive elements - Special formatting or CSS customization
In Blackboard: Link this prominently in the module, perhaps as a featured item students should see.






Screenshot needed: The Highlight Page as it renders inside Blackboard’s content viewer — ideally showing a case study or industry spotlight with an image and formatted text.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-highlight-page-rendered.png





8.5.2.3 Lecture Folders (Module [X] Lecture 1, Module [X] Lecture 2)
Your course typically includes two lectures per module. Each lecture folder contains three standardized components:






Screenshot needed: One lecture folder expanded inside Blackboard Ultra, showing the three items — Lecture Notes, Presentation, and Tutorial — with their respective file icons.
Temporary reference: See Lesley University’s Blackboard Quick Start Guide for an example of content item layout inside a folder.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-lecture-folder-expanded.png




8.5.2.3.1 Lecture Notes
Format: Detailed written notes covering all lecture content.
In Quarto: Create as a .qmd file with:
# Lecture 1: Foundational Concepts

## What You'll Learn
- Core principle 1
- Core principle 2
- Core principle 3

## Detailed Notes

### Section 1: Background
Provide context and explain why this matters...

### Section 2: Methodology
Step-by-step explanation...

### Section 3: Examples
Real-world applications...

## Key Takeaways
- Point 1
- Point 2
- Point 3
Advantage: Students can review the lecture asynchronously, search for specific content, and have a reference document throughout the course.


8.5.2.3.2 Presentation
Format: The slide presentation from your live lecture or a structured presentation of key concepts.
In Quarto: Either: - Export slides as static HTML for viewing in the browser - Include links to PDF versions of presentations - Create interactive HTML presentations using Quarto’s reveal.js integration
Tip: Always provide both presentation and notes. Some students learn better from visuals; others prefer reading detailed explanations.


8.5.2.3.3 Tutorial
Format: Hands-on, step-by-step guidance for applying the lecture concepts.
In Quarto: Create interactive tutorials with: - Code examples (with syntax highlighting) - Expected outputs - Common mistakes to avoid - Practice exercises
Example structure:
# Tutorial: Building Your First Interactive Visualization

## Prerequisites
- Completed Lecture 1 and 2
- R or Python installed

## Step 1: Set Up Your Environment
your code here

## Step 2: Load Your Data
...

## Practice Exercise
Now you try! [Exercise prompt]

## Solution
[Revealed after students attempt]



8.5.2.4 Deliverables Folder
This folder organizes all student assignments, labs, participation activities, and project milestones. Each item has an associated submission window.






Screenshot needed: The Deliverables folder expanded in Blackboard Ultra, showing all six item types — Assignment, Lab 1, Lab 2, Participation 1, Participation 2, and Project Step — with submission due dates visible.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-deliverables-folder-expanded.png




8.5.2.4.1 Assignment
Purpose: The primary assessment for the module, typically demonstrating mastery of core concepts.
Structure: - Prompt or problem statement - Submission requirements - Grading rubric - Due date with submission window - Optional: Example solutions or rubrics
In Quarto: Create as a .qmd file with clear sections:
# Module 3 Assignment: Data Visualization Project

## Overview
Create an interactive visualization that tells a story with data.

## Requirements
1. ...
2. ...
3. ...

## Submission
- Format: HTML file or link to published visualization
- Due: [Date] by 11:59 PM ET
- Submit via Blackboard assignment tool

## Grading Rubric
[Detailed rubric with point allocations]

## Resources
- [Link to tutorial]
- [Link to examples]
- [Link to tool documentation]
In Blackboard: Set up a Blackboard Assignment with: - Same due date and submission window - Link to the assignment prompt (your HTML) - Grading rubric embedded or linked - Late submission policy






Screenshot needed: The Blackboard Ultra assignment creation screen showing the due date field, submission window settings, rubric attachment, and the content link field where the HTML prompt URL is pasted.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-assignment-creation-screen.png





8.5.2.4.2 Labs (2 per module)
Purpose: Shorter, focused practice activities reinforcing specific skills.
Structure: - Clear objective - Guided steps - Verification or self-check mechanism
Example Topics: - Lab 1: SQL Query Practice - Lab 2: Debugging Common Errors
Submission: Either in-Blackboard submission or link to external system with submission tracking.






Screenshot needed: A lab item open in Blackboard showing the submission area — the text box, file attachment option, and submit button the student sees.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-lab-submission-view.png





8.5.2.4.3 Participation Activities (2 per module)
Purpose: Engagement activities that demonstrate attendance and involvement. These might include:
Types: - Discussion board posts (peer feedback) - Reflection prompts (metacognitive activities) - Peer review assignments - Quiz-style quick checks - Live session engagement recording
Structure: - Clear prompt - Expected participation level (e.g., “1 main post + 2 peer responses”) - Grading criteria - Submission deadline
In Blackboard: Implement as: - Discussion board forums - Assignment submissions (for reflections) - Quiz tool (for quick checks) - Attendance tracking (for synchronous sessions)
Tip: Vary participation types throughout the semester to maintain engagement and accommodate different learning styles.






Screenshot needed: A Blackboard Ultra discussion board showing a participation prompt with student reply threads visible (blur student names if needed).
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-participation-discussion-board.png





8.5.2.4.4 Project Steps (progressive milestones)
Purpose: For courses with semester-long or multi-module projects, break them into manageable steps with intermediate deadlines.
Structure: - Project step number (e.g., “Project Step 1: Proposal”) - What students should submit - Deliverables - Feedback timeline - Submission window
Example Project Progression: - Step 1: Project Proposal (Module 1) - Step 2: Data Analysis & Exploration (Module 3) - Step 3: Preliminary Results & Visualization (Module 6) - Step 4: Final Report & Presentation (Module 8)
Advantage: Milestones prevent students from leaving everything until the last minute and give you multiple checkpoints to provide feedback.






Screenshot needed: Project step submission in Blackboard showing the milestone title, point value, due date, and the submission window with “Opens” and “Due” dates both visible.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-project-step-submission-window.png







8.5.3 Workbook Exercise: Design Your Module Structure
Take time to plan your course structure before building it in Quarto and Blackboard.
Task: Map out your course modules and components.
Step 1: Decide how many modules you’ll have and what each covers
Module 1: _________________________________
Module 2: _________________________________
Module 3: _________________________________
[Continue for all modules]

├── Wrap-Up Content: [What will you overview?]
├── Highlight Page: [What special content/case study?]
├── Lecture 1: ___________________________
├── Lecture 2: ___________________________
├── Assignment: __________________________
├── Lab 1: ________________________________
├── Lab 2: ________________________________
├── Participation 1: _____________________
├── Participation 2: _____________________
└── Project Step: _________________________
Step 3: Count total deliverables

Number of lectures: _____
Number of assignments: _____
Number of labs: _____
Number of participation activities: _____

Verification Checklist: - [ ] All modules have defined content - [ ] Each module follows the consistent structure - [ ] Deliverable count is realistic for your course load - [ ] You have feedback capacity for all assessments - [ ] Project steps align with semester timeline (if applicable)

title: 8  Blackboard Ultra: Deploying Quarto Content to Your Course Shell
section: 8.6 Creating Content in Quarto for Blackboard Deployment
text:
8.6 Creating Content in Quarto for Blackboard Deployment
Now that you have your structure planned, let’s set up your Quarto project to generate deployable content.

8.6.1 Project Organization in Quarto
Create a Quarto project with folders that mirror your Blackboard structure:






Screenshot needed: VS Code or your editor showing the Quarto project folder tree — all module-01/ through module-N/ folders open with the subfolders lecture-1/, lecture-2/, and deliverables/ visible.
Replace with: images/ch07/quarto-project-folder-structure.png



your-course/
├── _quarto.yml
├── README.md
├── index.qmd
├── module-01/
│   ├── _module01.yml
│   ├── wrap-up.qmd
│   ├── highlight.qmd
│   ├── lecture-1/
│   │   ├── lecture-notes.qmd
│   │   ├── presentation.qmd
│   │   └── tutorial.qmd
│   ├── lecture-2/
│   │   ├── lecture-notes.qmd
│   │   ├── presentation.qmd
│   │   └── tutorial.qmd
│   └── deliverables/
│       ├── assignment.qmd
│       ├── lab-1.qmd
│       ├── lab-2.qmd
│       ├── participation-1.qmd
│       ├── participation-2.qmd
│       └── project-step-1.qmd
├── module-02/
│   └── [same structure]
└── images/
    ├── module-01/
    ├── module-02/
    └── [shared images]


8.6.2 Configuration: _quarto.yml
Set up your _quarto.yml to generate a website structure compatible with Blackboard:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: _site
  render:
    - "*.qmd"
    - "*/**.qmd"

website:
  title: "Your Course Title"
  site-url: "https://pages.github.com/your-course"
  repo-url: "https://github.com/your-org/your-course"
  repo-actions: [edit, issue, source]
  
  navbar:
    background: primary
    search: true
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - text: Course Modules
        menu:
          - href: module-01/wrap-up.qmd
            text: "Module 1"
          - href: module-02/wrap-up.qmd
            text: "Module 2"
          - text: "---"
          - text: Resources
          - href: resources.qmd
            text: Course Resources

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    css: custom.css
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-copy: true


8.6.3 Rendering and Packaging for Blackboard
When you’re ready to deploy content to Blackboard:






Screenshot needed: Terminal window showing the output of quarto render completing successfully — progress indicators, file counts, and the final “Output created: _site/” line.
Replace with: images/ch07/quarto-render-terminal-output.png



# Render all Quarto files to HTML
quarto render

# This creates the _site/ folder with all HTML, CSS, images, etc.
# Now package it for Blackboard upload
Creating _site.zip:
# On Windows (PowerShell)
Compress-Archive -Path _site -DestinationPath _site.zip

# On macOS/Linux
zip -r _site.zip _site/


8.6.4 Image References: Why They Persist
One important note about image links: When you reference images in your Quarto files and they’re included in _site.zip, Blackboard will import them along with the HTML. This means your image links will continue to work because:

Relative paths: If your HTML files reference images with relative paths (e.g., ../images/diagram.png), the image hierarchy is preserved
Content collection structure: Blackboard maintains the folder structure when you import _site.zip
Automatic linking: Blackboard’s content collection system updates internal references automatically

Important: Always use relative paths in your Quarto files, not absolute URLs:
❌ Don’t do this:
![Diagram](https://example.com/images/diagram.png)
✓ Do this:
![Diagram](../images/diagram.png)

8.7.2 Subsequent Updates: Semester Deployment
After your initial setup, updating content becomes straightforward:






Screenshot needed: Side-by-side of the content collection folder before and after re-uploading _site.zip — showing the “Date Modified” column updating to the new timestamp while links in Blackboard remain unchanged.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-content-collection-before-after-update.png




Make Changes in Quarto
Edit .qmd files as needed
Render and Package
quarto render
Compress-Archive -Path _site -DestinationPath _site.zip
Upload New _site.zip

Go to Blackboard Content Collection
Choose Upload Files and select your new _site.zip
Blackboard replaces old files with updated versions
Existing links automatically point to the updated content


Important Caveat: This replacement process works perfectly for content files (lectures, tutorials, materials). However, if you’re changing your module structure (adding/removing modules, reordering sections), you’ll need to create new links or adjust existing ones in Blackboard. This is why the initial setup with Andy is critical—lock in your structure before the semester starts.


8.7.3 Workbook Exercise: Prepare Your First Deployment
Task: Package your Quarto content for deployment.
Step 1: Verify your Quarto project structure
# Navigate to your course folder
cd /path/to/your-course

# List contents to verify structure
Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Filter "*.qmd" | Select-Object FullName
Step 2: Render all content
# Render your Quarto project
quarto render

# Verify _site folder was created
Test-Path _site
Step 3: Create deployment package
# Create _site.zip
Compress-Archive -Path _site -DestinationPath _site.zip -Force

# Verify the archive was created
Get-Item _site.zip | Select-Object Name, Length
Step 4: Prepare deployment checklist

Quarto project renders without errors
All .qmd files convert to .html successfully
Images and assets included in _site/
_site.zip created successfully

text:
8.8 Best Practices for Quarto + Blackboard Integration

8.8.1 File Naming Conventions
Use clear, consistent naming that makes sense in both Quarto file systems and Blackboard:
✓ Good: - module-01-intro.qmd - lecture-1-foundations.qmd - assignment-project-proposal.qmd - lab-01-sql-basics.qmd
❌ Avoid: - module1.qmd - lecture.qmd - assignment.qmd - lab.qmd


8.8.2 URL/Link Strategy
When creating cross-links in your course materials:
Linking within Blackboard: Use relative paths that Blackboard can resolve:
See [this tutorial](../lecture-1/tutorial.html)
Linking to external resources: Use full URLs:
Check out [this guide](https://example.com/guide)
Linking to assignments: Reference Blackboard assignment links:
Submit your work via [this Blackboard assignment link](https://blackboard.bu.edu/webapps/assignment/...)


8.8.3 Maintenance During the Semester
Content Updates Without Redeployment: - Small text corrections: Fix in Quarto, render, and re-upload _site.zip - Broken links: Test regularly, fix in Quarto, re-render - Typos in assignments: Fix immediately and update within 24 hours
When NOT to Re-upload _site.zip: - After students have already submitted work on an assignment (the submission date might change if Blackboard refreshes) - If you’re only updating one file (upload it individually instead) - During peak submission periods (upload during off-hours to avoid conflicts)
Communication with Students: - If you make significant content changes, post an announcement: “Updated Module 3 lecture notes—please review the latest version” - Include version numbers in assignment prompts: “Submit your response to Assignment 3 (v1.1)”


8.8.4 Disaster Recovery and Rollback
Because your Quarto files are in version control (Git):

Keep previous deployments: Don’t delete old _site.zip files
Tag stable versions: In Git, tag releases after successful deployments
git tag -a v1.0-spring-2026 -m "Spring 2026 course - first deployment"
Rollback if needed: If something breaks, restore from the previous version:
git checkout v1.0-spring-2026
quarto render
Compress-Archive -Path _site -DestinationPath _site.zip
# Upload the previous version to Blackboard

text:
8.11 Troubleshooting Common Issues

8.11.1 Problem: Links Break After Uploading to Blackboard
Cause: Absolute URLs in Quarto files or incorrect relative paths
Solution: - Check all image and link references in your .qmd files - Use relative paths: ../images/logo.png not /images/logo.png - Update any absolute URLs to relative paths - Re-render and re-upload


8.11.2 Problem: Images Don’t Display in Blackboard
Cause: Images not included in _site/ folder or path references are wrong
Solution: - Ensure images are in a subfolder of your Quarto project (e.g., images/) - Verify Quarto rendering includes images in _site/ - Check that _site.zip includes image files - Verify relative paths in HTML match actual image locations


8.11.3 Problem: Content Collection Upload Fails
Cause: File size too large or connection interrupted
Solution: - If _site.zip is very large (>1GB), compress images bash   # Reduce image quality in Quarto with CSS   img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; } - Try uploading individual files instead of _site.zip - Contact Andy if technical issues persist


8.11.4 Problem: Students See Old Content After Re-upload
Cause: Browser caching
Solution: - Instruct students to clear browser cache or use Ctrl+Shift+Delete - Use browser-specific cache-busting in _quarto.yml - Announce updates in course announcements


8.11.5 Problem: Module Structure Changes Required Mid-Semester
Cause: Didn’t lock structure with Andy before launch
Solution: - Contact Andy before making structural changes - Update both Quarto and Blackboard simultaneously - Communicate changes to students - Test thoroughly before making live

10 Supercharging Course Production with GitHub Copilot & AI Tools
As an educator building out reproducible courses with Quarto, you are inherently writing code. Whether it is Markdown for lecture notes, Python or R for your data analysis labs, or simply writing Python scripts to automate your directory structures, you are functioning as a developer.
To assist you in this endeavor, GitHub Copilot serves as an indispensable AI pair programmer. Copilot is integrated directly into your IDE (like Antigravity or VS Code) and provides real-time autocomplete suggestions, conversational assistance, and code generation.

title: 9  Automating with AI
text:
10.1 The AI Editor Stack
For day-to-day course production, the most practical setup is a combination of:

GitHub for repositories, version history, and publishing
GitHub Copilot for inline suggestions and chat support while writing code or Quarto
GitHub Classroom for distributing coding assignments and collecting student work
VS Code or Antigravity when using ChatGPT-connected coding tools for drafting scripts, revising content, and debugging
Cursor when project-wide AI editing and rapid refactoring are useful
Gemini and OpenAI APIs when you want scripts to generate metadata, summaries, or structured course assets at scale

Used together, these tools reduce setup time, help standardize materials, and make it easier to maintain course content across semesters.

title: 9  Automating with AI
text:
10.3 Copilot for Course Organization Scripts
One of the most powerful uses of Copilot is automating the tedious parts of course administration.
For instance, consider the requirement of generating a consistent module structure (M0, M1) complete with boilerplate Quarto files for Lecture Notes, Labs, and Assignments. Instead of writing this entire generation script from scratch, you can provide Copilot with a prompt outlining your needs:

“Write a Python script that generates directories named M0 through M7. Inside each directory, create the following empty Quarto files: _LN1.qmd, _LN2.qmd, _P1.qmd, _P2.qmd, _Lab1.qmd, _Lab2.qmd, _A.qmd, _Proj.qmd, _Part1.qmd, and _Part2.qmd.”

Copilot will swiftly generate a generate_modules.py script that perfectly matches your requirements.

10.3.1 The Module Generation Script
If you don’t want to use Copilot to generate it, you can simply copy the script below. Create a file named generate_modules.py in your project root, paste the following code, and run it via terminal (python generate_modules.py):
import os
import sys
import shutil

NUM_MODULES = 8  # M0 - M7

def load_course_title(base_path):
    config_path = os.path.join(base_path, ".course_config.env")
    if not os.path.exists(config_path):
        return ""

    with open(config_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        for line in f:
            line = line.strip()
            if not line or line.startswith("#") or "=" not in line:
                continue
            key, value = line.split("=", 1)
            if key.strip() == "COURSE_TITLE":
                return value.strip().strip('"').strip("'")
    return ""

def make_qmd(path, title):
    with open(path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        f.write(f"---\ntitle: \"{title}\"\n---\n\n# {title}\n")

def main():
    base_path = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "."
    base_path = os.path.abspath(base_path)

    if not os.path.isdir(base_path):
        print(f"❌ Path does not exist: {base_path}")
        sys.exit(1)

    force_reset = "--force" in sys.argv
    print(f"📂 Using project root: {base_path}")
    course_title = load_course_title(base_path)

    for i in range(NUM_MODULES):
        module = f"M{i}"
        module_path = os.path.join(base_path, module)

        if os.path.exists(module_path) and not force_reset:
            print(f"❌ Module folder already exists: {module_path}")
            print("👉 Run with --force to overwrite everything.")
            sys.exit(1)

        if force_reset and os.path.isdir(module_path):
            shutil.rmtree(module_path)

        os.makedirs(module_path, exist_ok=True)

        qmd_files = {
            "Lecture Note 1": f"{module}_LN1.qmd",
            "Lecture Note 2": f"{module}_LN2.qmd",
            "Presentation 1": f"{module}_P1.qmd",
            "Presentation 2": f"{module}_P2.qmd",
            "Lab 1": f"{module}_Lab1.qmd",
            "Lab 2": f"{module}_Lab2.qmd",
            "Assignment": f"{module}_A.qmd",
            "Project": f"{module}_Proj.qmd",
            "Participation 1": f"{module}_Part1.qmd",
            "Participation 2": f"{module}_Part2.qmd",
        }

        for label, fname in qmd_files.items():
            file_path = os.path.join(module_path, fname)
            page_title = course_title if course_title else f"{module} {label}"
            make_qmd(file_path, page_title)

        os.makedirs(os.path.join(module_path, "data"), exist_ok=True)
        os.makedirs(os.path.join(module_path, f"{module}_lecture01_figures"), exist_ok=True)
        os.makedirs(os.path.join(module_path, f"{module}_lecture02_figures"), exist_ok=True)
        os.makedirs(os.path.join(module_path, "output"), exist_ok=True)

    print("✅ All module folders and QMD skeletons generated.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

title: 9  Automating with AI
text:
10.4 Automating Metadata with LLMs (OpenAI / Gemini)
Beyond structural generation, you can use large language models (LLMs) via APIs to automatically populate the YAML metadata in your Quarto files. For example, a script can read your course outline (an Excel or CSV file) and prompt an AI to generate SEO-friendly keywords and summaries for each lecture note.

10.4.1 Setting up API Keys (.env)
Before running API-dependent scripts, you must securely store your API keys. Never hardcode your keys into your Python scripts or commit them to GitHub. Instead, use a .env file.
Create a file named .env in the root of your project directory and add the following placeholder keys. Replace your_... with your actual keys.
# .env

# OpenAI API Key (Required for GPT-4 integrations)
OPENAI_API_KEY="your_openai_api_key_here"

# Gemini API Key (Required if you use Google's models instead)
GEMINI_API_KEY="your_gemini_api_key_here"

[!WARNING] Ensure your .gitignore file contains the line .env so your keys are not accidentally uploaded to your GitHub repository!



10.4.2 The Metadata Population Script
Once your keys are configured, you can use the following script to automatically fill in the YAML frontmatter for your .qmd files. This script uses the OpenAI API to generate keywords and a short summary based on a provided CSV outline.
Save this file as file_qmd_metadata.py:
import os
import sys
import pandas as pd
from datetime import date
import openai 
import random
from dotenv import load_dotenv

# Load the keys from our .env file!
load_dotenv()

def generate_ai_content(title, subtitle, description, use_openai=True):
    keywords = ["Cloud", "Big Data", "Analytics", "Computing"]
    summary = description if description else "Short description."
    
    if use_openai and "OPENAI_API_KEY" in os.environ:
        try:
            openai.api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
            prompt = f"Generate 4 keywords and a 2-line summary for:\nTitle: {title}\nSubtitle: {subtitle}\nDescription: {description}"
            resp = openai.ChatCompletion.create(
                model="gpt-4o-mini",
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
                max_tokens=150,
                temperature=0.7
            )
            text = resp["choices"][0]["message"]["content"].strip()
            parts = text.split("\n")
            keywords = parts[0].replace("Keywords:", "").split(",") if parts else keywords
            summary = " ".join(parts[1:]) if len(parts) > 1 else summary
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"⚠️ AI generation failed, using defaults: {e}")
    else:
        words = title.split()
        keywords = random.sample(words, min(4, len(words)))
        summary = description[:150] if description else "Short description."
    
    return [kw.strip() for kw in keywords], summary

# Helper functions omitted for brevity (e.g. get_row_value, load_course_title)

def write_yaml(file_path, row, course_title=""):
    title = row.get("Title", "Course Content")
    subtitle = row.get("Subtitle", "")
    desc = row.get("Description", "")
    
    keywords, summary = generate_ai_content(title, subtitle, desc)

    yaml_lines = [
        "---",
        f'title: "{title}"',
        f'subtitle: "{subtitle}"',
        "number-sections: true",
        f'date: "{date.today()}"',
        f'categories: {keywords}',
        f'description: "{summary}"',
        "---"
    ]

    with open(file_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        f.write("\n".join(yaml_lines) + "\n")
    print(f"✅ Filled {file_path}")

def main():
    if len(sys.argv) < 2:
        print("Usage: python file_qmd_metadata.py <outline.csv>")
        sys.exit(1)

    input_file = sys.argv[1]
    df = pd.read_csv(input_file)
    
    for _, row in df.iterrows():
        module = row.get("Module")
        filetype = row.get("FileType")
        fpath = os.path.join(".", module, f"{module}_{filetype}.qmd")

        if os.path.exists(fpath):
            write_yaml(fpath, row)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

title: 9  Automating with AI
text:
10.5 Copilot for Authoring Quarto Content
Copilot is not just for Python scripts. Because Quarto is a text-based format (.qmd), Copilot understands the context of your lecture notes and can predict what you want to write next.

Generating Code Blocks: If you are writing a lab assignment and need to insert a code block showing how to plot a scatterplot using matplotlib or ggplot2, simply write the comment # create a scatterplot of x vs y and Copilot will suggest the exact code block.
Writing Summaries: You can highlight a large chunk of text in your lecture notes and ask Copilot Chat to “Summarize this section for the introduction.”
Translating Content: If you are moving an old syllabus from Word to Quarto Markdown, you can prompt Copilot to convert your plain text into structured Markdown tables and formatted lists.

title: 9  Automating with AI
text:
10.6 Responsible AI Use in Education
While GitHub Copilot is a powerful authoring assistant for you as an educator, it also introduces important discussions regarding responsible use in the classroom.
If you are using GitHub Classroom (discussed in Chapter 6), your students may also have access to Copilot through GitHub Education. Verified students can get free access to GitHub Copilot, and Copilot Chat can be used with repository context to answer questions about the codebase. It is critical to establish a clear policy in your syllabus regarding the acceptable use of Generative AI.

[!TIP] Use Copilot to help you generate boilerplate code, format markdown, and troubleshoot errors. However, always review the generated content critically before publishing it to your Blackboard course shell to ensure pedagogical accuracy.

Integrating AI into your workflow does not replace your expertise; it simply accelerates the mechanics of course production, giving you more time to focus on teaching.

title: 9  Automating with AI
section: 10.7 Integrating These Tools in an Analytics Class
text:
10.7 Integrating These Tools in an Analytics Class
In an analytics class, these tools can speed up the parts of course design that are repetitive but still technical. GitHub and GitHub Classroom help organize materials, distribute assignments, and keep submissions structured. GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and ChatGPT-connected coding tools help draft scripts, clean up Quarto files, troubleshoot issues, and generate boilerplate more quickly.
For larger automation tasks, OpenAI or Gemini APIs can be used from Python scripts to generate metadata, summaries, or other structured content. Remotion and Manim can support technical video creation, while Hugging Face can support model experimentation or institution-scale AI workflows. The main value is not replacing instructional judgment, but reducing the time spent on setup, formatting, and iteration.
